sentences.text: My name is Sam Carmel, C-A-R-M-E-L.
Szyja Karmiol, S-Z-Y-J-A.
Karmiol is K-A-R-M-I-O-L
The 9 the 15 1916.
The city Pinczow, Swietokrzyskie, [?
Gubernd ?] Wojewodztwo.
Poland.
I was three months old when my mother passed away.
I was a year and five months old when my father passed away.
And we remain seven brothers and one sister.
My mother's name is Sara Karmiol.
From the home she was [?
Midlash. ?] And my father's name is Tobiasz Karmiol.
I-- and we were eight brothers and one sister.
They-- family want to take us apart.
Everybody want to take a child, so my oldest brother, about 16 years old, he said no.
We are going to live together.
If I'm going to live, we're going to live.
If we're going to die, we're all going to die together, but I don't give any child away.
And he was working very hard, and my sister became the housekeeper.
Szabsa was the oldest brother's name, and Hersz Lieb was the second brother's name.
Abraham was the third brother's name.
Then came my sister Alta Karmiol.
Then came Jonas Karmiol, then came Jopsh Karmiol, then Modcha, then me.
I was three months old when my mother passed away.
I was a year and five months old when my father passed away.
We are eight children.
My sister was eight years old, and she was cooking.
She couldn't reach to the stove, so my brother made a stool.
And she was standing on that stool, and she could reach the kitchen.
And she was working very hard.
And she was cooking, and cleaning, and everything that has to be done.
And she grew up and we didn't have much to eat, actually.
She cooked a big pot with potato soup.
She know the-- less the money, but they just was cheapest.
So we ate, we were hungry, after we ate even.
And then life goes on.
And when my cousin from America came, Kalman Karmiol-- Carmel, he came to Pinczow, and he saw what is going on.
He gave us money to know that we should live better.
Should-- we should have a better-- live.
And he, he helped the whole family.
He was sending every time money for us to have, what to live.
But was a big family, though.
He could send only so much.
So we had it better than we had before because we had some money.
And then my sister, he gave her a, a dowry to only girls from the family, and they should be able to get married.
And my sister got married to a very nice guy.
He was dealing with grains.
I was going to the Polish school and I was going to the cheder.
Yeah.
But the--
Yeah.
Yeah.
We-- the older brother, he would-- about leading us with that religion.
We had to go to the schul.
We had to pray every day.
We had to pray on Saturday special.
Our holidays, we were in the schul.
And they, they kept me in cheder.
Even they didn't have no money, but they figure out so that it should last for everything, the money.
And we would go down--
Little boy, I was trying to help my sister whatever I could.
She made a little bundle.
She went to the river because the water was-- a lot of water to bring from the place where it was hard to carry.
So it was easier to take the laundry to the river.
She made that small bundle for me to help her carry because I couldn't carry.
I have a bundle.
I was young.
And-- but I wanted help every time my sister.
And it was hard life, very hard life.
But we got-- we, we are used to it like this, and we finished.
We thought it should be.
And when my sister got married, she moved to another city with her husband, and her mother was living with her.
The mother was stealing grains from my-- from her son and bringing that grains to her daughter.
And I saw that, and I didn't want to make a fight between my sister and her husband/ so I went and I told my brother-in-law that-- what she is doing.
He slapped me and threw me out from the house.
And I, I, I didn't have there go so I went to Cracow that time.
11 Years old.
By myself.
I went on a horse and wagon, what they brought eggs, and chickens, and gooses to Cracow to sell.
So I went on that wagon.
We went two, two nights and one day.
We were driving to Cracow from that city.
They were trying to make a work.
My oldest brother and the next to oldest brother, they were also dealing with grains, buying from the [INAUDIBLE] and selling to the-- to a big company the grain.
And they make the flour from the grains, you know, and everything else.
Yeah.
They were all doing that.
They all help.
They put in the-- little bit help that should be able to work.
When my sister got married, I left to-- I left with her.
I went with her to Wieliczka.
And they but help me with their mother-in-law still.
And he slapped me and threw me out from the house.
And I didn't have where to go, so I went to Cracow to look for a job.
I look like I'm seven years old and I-- because I was short and skinny.
So I said, I'm older than I look.
I am already 11 years old, and I want a job.
They offered me food.
I was hungry, but still I said, no.
I'm not going to take any food unless you going to give me a job.
So they-- who's going to hire a kid to work?
Finally, I went so far till I found a man who was very intelligent, and he listened to me from where I coming from and everything.
I told him the story that I haven't got where to stay.
I didn't-- my brother threw me out.
I told him the whole thing, the truth.
And he said, OK, take a-- I sent for food to-- you should eat something.
I'm sure you're hungry.
I said, I wouldn't eat unless you give me a job.
He said, take the broom and sweep the floor, and I moved away everything from the corners.
And I said, oh, it's so good that I-- he should give me the job.
Then I finished sweeping.
He says, now you're going to eat.
OK, now I'm going to eat.
The first time in my life I had food enough what I wasn't hungry no more.
Eggs-- he gave me whole-- he sent into a restaurant and they brought in all kind of food-- bread, and butter, and eggs, and salad, and everything you-- tomatoes.
And I was-- the first time I was-- ate till I not hungry no more.
Yeah.
It's was Shayek--
Mine--
Anyway, he was-- oh, my God.
How, how can I not remember?
And I was working for him till the war came, the Germans.
I was-- I was first a delivery boy.
He sent me with-- to deliver this, to deliver that, to bring something in, to help out, whatever I-- I was doing everything.
And with all my heart, with all my ego, you know, to work hard and nice and he should like me, he shouldn't fire me.
He gave me 20 zlotys a month.
This was $2 a month.
I made myself a budget that I have to spend $5 for a room.
Then I remain 15 zlotys. 15 zlotys-- three zlotys I have to put away in case I need a pair pants, or shoes, or a shirt, or a, a haircut.
So those three zlotys I save for those things.
I can only spend 12 zlotys a month for food.
This is 40 groszy a day.
I said I have-- I can have to split it 40 groszy, but, but two times a day to eat to survive.
So I ate, but I didn't have enough.
But I survived.
I had let's see, bread with soup, or a bread and butter and a coffee.
I couldn't afford a better meal-- you understand-- at that money.
But I was-- I was going along because I-- I'm used to be hungry.
In Cracow.
Oh, in, in a-- in a private home where I slept with somebody.
That why I got so cheap, five zlotys a month.
So as long I can go buy.
In a family, yeah.
And I was working.
Then he put me as a salesman.
I got myself to a salesman, and I was working as a salesman.
When I was about 15 years old, I became the salesman.
And I worked so-- and, and so-- and I was good to the customers.
They had the linings for the-- for the suits and coats.
And they had other, like, aspirins, cotton.
They delivered this to the pharmacies.
So I delivered this, and I helped packing and everything.
And then I became-- I, I got sick.
I got very sick.
I had the operation here on my-- here, the throat.
I couldn't swallow, you know.
A growth in there.
And I had the operation in stepped in a very big complications.
And the doctors couldn't bring down the temperature.
I had a long time temperature.
So they give up, practically, on me.
The doctors, they called one of my brothers.
Somebody from the family should be with me in case I pass away.
In that time, my mother came to my dream.
And she blew away with  her apron the temperature.
And she said to me, don't worry.
You're going to be strong, and you're going to live a long life.
And it just happened after that she-- and she disappeared.
And I was looking for her.
What happened to her?
Then I finally-- I woke up, and my brother was by my bed.
He was staying there.
And I told him about my dream.
And he said to me, yes, this-- and I explained how she looked like because I didn't have even a picture from her how she looked like.
He said, yes, this is our mother.
And he was a couple days with me, and I got better and better.
After this, my temperature went away right after she left.
And I got better and better, and then
I went out from the hospital.
I went back where I was living, and then I went to work.
I have to make a living.
So I start working.
Boss says to me, no, put it down.
I start crying.
What I'm going to do now?
Why you sending me away?
He said, I'm not sending you away.
I'm sending you for vacation.
You're going to go for vacation for two weeks.
Here are the ticket, and here is the address where you're going to go.
And you're going to-- after two weeks, you're going to come back to work.
And I did.
And I felt much better.
He was very good to me.
Yeah, a Jewish man.
Kislowitz.
Now I remind myself the name.
Kislowitz, his name, Abraham Kislowitz.
Abraham wasn't living no more, but his two sons were running the business
After I came back, I was working, then one of those days he says to me, you're going to become the manager.
I said, how can I become manager?
He has been working-- they can be my grandfathers.
How can I manage them?
He said, you're going to tell them what to do.
And they going to-- they going to listen to you.
And if they don't listen, don't argue with them.
But tell me that they are not doing what you are asking them to do.
And I didn't want it, but I have no choice.
I had to do it, whatever he tells me because he trusted me with everything.
And sure enough, he went for vacation, the boss.
And he says, you take care the registers.
Nobody else goes to the register.
When it comes to get money, pay out money, you are doing.
You have our receipts, and you can run it.
And I was running it, and I was good.
Then came 1939, when the Germans start coming into Poland.
Yes, I had friends, of course.
Most Jewish friends because the Poles, they were antisemites.
Antisemites.
They didn't like-- the fact is, they try to come-- they came to the pack.
They were-- a bunch of them, they start hitting us in the pack.
So we, we run away, or we gave them back.
If we were to-- we felt we can fight them, we would fight back.
Anyway--
1939, the Germans came in to Cracow.
And they were marching the Jews from Czechoslovakia and from Austria.
They, they marched them in the streets from Cracow, and I saw they are dirty and hungry.
I went into the bakery.
I bought eight breads.
I couldn't carry more.
And I went out to-- handed them the bread.
They should have at least have a piece of bread.
So one bread of they ate fell down on the floor because I throw it at them because the guard didn't let me close to them, to hand it to them.
So one bread fell down and one of the Jews bent down to pick up the bread.
And the guard hit him with his rifle.
And this burned me up so I at the time I couldn't-- I would-- If I had something in my hand I would kill this guy because he did that to him.
And so then my boss had a revolver.
So I asked him he should give me the revolver.
He says, you, a revolver?
I can't believe it.
What's the matter?
Come on in the office.
I went in the office and I explained to him that I made up my mind.
I'm going to go in, in their headquarters, take out the revolver, how many I can kill, I will kill.
And then I will have a paper with me in case I get killed that I did this because they-- in protest what they doing to our Jewish people.
In the headquarters, their headquarters.
The commanders from the--
Army, from all of them.
Germany.
Germans.
In their headquarters.
So--
And what did you have on the piece of paper?
I written down that I am sacrificing my life because of that purpose, because they are treating our Jewish people wrong, and they should try to not to be murderers to them.
And, and he says to me, it's very nice of you, very noble, that you want to give away your young life for that purpose.
He said, if we would have thousands like you, then we could do something.
But you the only one.
You're going to go in, you're going to make that thing first because they're going to take out hundreds or thousands of Jews and kill them for this, like you killed their leader.
I beg you, don't do that.
Don't do that.
You don't want to be on your conscience in case you remain alive, that because of you, they killed so many Jews.
That's what stopped me.
And I said, I wouldn't be able to look at it, what they doing to the people, and I have to leave here.
He said, OK, I don't want you to leave.
But you want to leave, I'm not going to keep you back.
Leave.
And I went-- made up my mind to go to Russia to escape Cracow, just go to Russia.
[INAUDIBLE] And on the way to Russia--
I was in that time 30-- no, '39 is it-- I was 15 years old, something, about 16--
Anyway, I went to-- I went to go to-- I went on the train to go to Russian border.
Then the Germans came on the train to take us off from the train where I was going to leave--
Yeah, I told him.
I said, I cannot live here anymore.
I cannot look at that, because I'm going to bust.
[INAUDIBLE].
So he says, OK, leave.
And I-- he was crying that I left, because he was to me like a partner, not like a boss.
Anyway, I-- the Germans came on the train, so I jumped from the train.
I didn't want them to catch me while the train was going.
On the train?
About two, two days, because I jumped on the train and from the train.
Otherwise it wouldn't take me so long.
But I was jumping on the train back.
And--
I had whatever I could carry with me.
Some clothes, that's all what I had.
It was already end of the year, by November.
About starting winter already.
Cold.
And then November/December.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Somebody there?
And I went--
On the train, jumping off--
Jumping off the train, on the train.
Finally, I arrived in Lviv.
In Lviv, they, they, they took--
Lviv, Lemberg, Lviv.
This is in-- the Russians took a part from Poland.
Used to be Poland, but that time Russia already there.
So they took me to a coal mine in Donbas.
The Russians, and they sent me to Donbas to work in a coal mine.
OK, in beginning they were treating us nice.
Then they were working the coal mine, we--
With other people who were from-- like me.
They took all of them, and they sent us to--
Young boys, older boys, whatever they could they--
Girls, too.
They took us and they send us to Donbas.
And there-- and that time they were working hard in the coal mine, and we didn't what nothing to eat.
We came in in the-- in the restaurant there to eat, and there was a soup, a piece of cabbage was swimming in the soup.
But they give us bread.
If we were giving eight tons coal a day, we were getting 800 grams of bread a day.
But beside this, we-- once a week we had-- or a piece of fish or piece of meat, once a week.
And the rest of the week we had only--
In Donbasm Voroshilovgrad.
This is the-- this is-- Donbas was the coal mine.
With I and digging-- knock out the coal from the walls.
Knock out, this I was doing.
They showed us what to do, and we were doing.
Anyway--
Oh, they had to special barracks for us to, to live in it.
Yeah, I had the friends that we went together.
We had friends.
But anyway, then one time came down, the main engineer from the coal mine.
And I was working-- I was a hard-working man.
I don't fool around with the work.
I do work.
So he saw me working and everything.
And I-- the time should go by, I was singing Jewish songs.
What were you singing?
What songs would you sing?
Oh, [SPEAKING HEBREW], you know--
[SINGING IN HEBREW] Oh, I forget already.
[SINGING IN HEBREW]
So he heard me singing.
And he says to me, I want to see you in my house.
I have very important.
And I went to his house.
And he said that I going to help you getting out from this coal mine, because sooner or later you're going to die here.
